pages.title: Mastering the Singleton Pattern
pages.subtitle: Introduce the topic and set expectations for the student.
pages.body:
What's up geeks and welcome to the channel In the first video of this series we are going
 to start with one of the simplest yet one of the most used design
 patterns out there: The Singleton Pattern The singleton is a creational design pattern
 this pattern ensures that only one instance of its kind exists
 and provides a single point of access to it from any other part of our application
 As a real-world analogy take the government of a country or its president,
 you see a given country can have only one official government or one official president
 regardless of the individuals who form this government or the person that
 is currently the president of this country, so the title "the government or the president
 of X" is a global identifier it is a global point of access
 used to represent or reference the president or the individuals in charge of this country
keyTakeaways: What's up geeks and welcome to the channel

In the first video of this series we are going
 to start with one of the simplest yet one of the most used design
 patterns out there: The Singleton Pattern

The singleton is a creational design pattern
 this pattern ensures that only one instance of its kind exists
 and provides a single point of access to it from any other part of our application
 As a real-world analogy take the government of a country or its president,
 you see a given country can have only one official government or one official president
 regardless of the individuals who form this government or the person that
 is currently the president of this country, so the title "the government or the president
 of X" is a global identifier it is a global point of access
 used to represent or reference the president or the individuals in charge of this country

Singletons represent exactly the same idea, just like global variables they let you access
 your object from anywhere in your application but on top of that, they encapsulate the
 attributes of this object in one single class and guarantee that only one instance of this class
 will be available at any point in time but why would anyone want to control
 how many instances a class has
pages.misconceptionFix: Do not memorize only the words. Understand why this concept is used and where it can fail.
pages.didYouKnow: What's up geeks and welcome to the channel
pages.example:
Singletons represent exactly the same idea, just like global variables they let you access
 your object from anywhere in your application but on top of that, they encapsulate the
 attributes of this object in one single class and guarantee that only one instance of this class
 will be available at any point in time but why would anyone want to control
 how many instances a class has

quiz.question: What is the main idea of "Mastering the Singleton Pattern"?
quiz.answer: What's up geeks and welcome to the channel
quiz.hint: Look at the source reference attached to this page.

 your object from anywhere in your application but on top of that, they encapsulate the

 will be available at any point in time but why would anyone want to control

pages.title: What is the Singleton Pattern?
pages.subtitle: Define the pattern and understand its core purpose (single instance guarantee).
pages.body:
and to these people I say you're right To solve this issue and as discussed
 in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap
 our if-statement with a synchronized block Now every time a thread tries to
 create an instance of the Singleton class it will have to wait for its turn as one thread
 only may execute this code block at a given time Okay with this we solved the threading issue
 however we added unnecessary overhead to the creation process of our singleton object
keyTakeaways: and to these people I say you're right

To solve this issue and as discussed
 in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap
 our if-statement with a synchronized block Now every time a thread tries to
 create an instance of the Singleton class it will have to wait for its turn as one thread
 only may execute this code block at a given time

Okay with this we solved the threading issue
 however we added unnecessary overhead to the creation process of our singleton object

You see after the single instance is created, when the threads of our application try to
 access it they will first have to wait for their turn to acquire the lock on our class
 and only when it is their time to go they will check if the instance
 is available and then return with it This problem is actually not specific
 to the Singleton pattern and can be solved by applying
 the double-checked locking idiom on our method How do we do that
pages.misconceptionFix: Do not memorize only the words. Understand why this concept is used and where it can fail.
pages.didYouKnow: and to these people I say you're right
pages.example:
You see after the single instance is created, when the threads of our application try to
 access it they will first have to wait for their turn to acquire the lock on our class
 and only when it is their time to go they will check if the instance
 is available and then return with it This problem is actually not specific
 to the Singleton pattern and can be solved by applying
 the double-checked locking idiom on our method How do we do that

pages.title: Singleton in the Real World
pages.subtitle: Use analogies (Government, Database) to solidify the concept.
pages.body:
now marked as volatile so every time we access
 this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
 So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user To avoid doing this and access the
 memory only once what we can do is
 store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
 then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
 to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory
keyTakeaways:
now marked as volatile so every time we access
 this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
 So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user

To avoid doing this and access the
 memory only once what we can do is
 store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
 then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement

The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
 to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory

This simple usage of such a local variable
 can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
 So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
 instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
 of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
 This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
 However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs
 to be adapted to handle multiple threads
pages.misconceptionFix: Do not memorize only the words. Understand why this concept is used and where it can fail.

 So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user
pages.example:
This simple usage of such a local variable
 can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
 So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
 instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
 of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
 This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
 However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs
 to be adapted to handle multiple threads

pages.title: Chapter 2: Thread Safety and Performance
pages.subtitle: Transition to advanced topics: handling multi-threading issues.
pages.body:
now marked as volatile so every time we access
 this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
 So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user To avoid doing this and access the
 memory only once what we can do is
 store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
 then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
 to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory
keyTakeaways:
now marked as volatile so every time we access
 this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
 So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user

To avoid doing this and access the
 memory only once what we can do is
 store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
 then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement

The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
 to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory

This simple usage of such a local variable
 can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
 So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
 instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
 of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
 This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
 However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs
 to be adapted to handle multiple threads
pages.misconceptionFix: Do not memorize only the words. Understand why this concept is used and where it can fail.

 this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached

 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user
pages.example:
This simple usage of such a local variable
 can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
 So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
 instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
 of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
 This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
 However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs

pages.title: Solving Race Conditions: Synchronization
pages.subtitle: Understand why synchronization is necessary in multi-threaded environments.
pages.body:
What's up geeks and welcome to the channel In the first video of this series we are going
 to start with one of the simplest yet one of the most used design
 patterns out there: The Singleton Pattern The singleton is a creational design pattern
 this pattern ensures that only one instance of its kind exists
 and provides a single point of access to it from any other part of our application
 As a real-world analogy take the government of a country or its president,
 you see a given country can have only one official government or one official president
 regardless of the individuals who form this government or the person that
 is currently the president of this country, so the title "the government or the president
 of X" is a global identifier it is a global point of access
 used to represent or reference the president or the individuals in charge of this country
keyTakeaways: What's up geeks and welcome to the channel

In the first video of this series we are going
 to start with one of the simplest yet one of the most used design
 patterns out there: The Singleton Pattern

The singleton is a creational design pattern
 this pattern ensures that only one instance of its kind exists
 and provides a single point of access to it from any other part of our application
 As a real-world analogy take the government of a country or its president,
 you see a given country can have only one official government or one official president
 regardless of the individuals who form this government or the person that
 is currently the president of this country, so the title "the government or the president
 of X" is a global identifier it is a global point of access
 used to represent or reference the president or the individuals in charge of this country

Singletons represent exactly the same idea, just like global variables they let you access
 your object from anywhere in your application but on top of that, they encapsulate the
 attributes of this object in one single class and guarantee that only one instance of this class
 will be available at any point in time but why would anyone want to control
 how many instances a class has
pages.misconceptionFix: Do not memorize only the words. Understand why this concept is used and where it can fail.

pages.title: Optimization 1: Double-Checked Locking
pages.subtitle: Implement the double-checked locking idiom to improve performance.
pages.body:
and to these people I say you're right To solve this issue and as discussed
 in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap
 our if-statement with a synchronized block Now every time a thread tries to
 create an instance of the Singleton class it will have to wait for its turn as one thread
 only may execute this code block at a given time Okay with this we solved the threading issue
 however we added unnecessary overhead to the creation process of our singleton object
keyTakeaways: and to these people I say you're right

To solve this issue and as discussed
 in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap
 our if-statement with a synchronized block Now every time a thread tries to
 create an instance of the Singleton class it will have to wait for its turn as one thread
 only may execute this code block at a given time

Okay with this we solved the threading issue
 however we added unnecessary overhead to the creation process of our singleton object

You see after the single instance is created, when the threads of our application try to
 access it they will first have to wait for their turn to acquire the lock on our class
 and only when it is their time to go they will check if the instance
 is available and then return with it This problem is actually not specific
 to the Singleton pattern and can be solved by applying
 the double-checked locking idiom on our method How do we do that
pages.misconceptionFix: Do not memorize only the words. Understand why this concept is used and where it can fail.
pages.didYouKnow: and to these people I say you're right
pages.example:
You see after the single instance is created, when the threads of our application try to
 access it they will first have to wait for their turn to acquire the lock on our class
 and only when it is their time to go they will check if the instance
 is available and then return with it This problem is actually not specific
 to the Singleton pattern and can be solved by applying
 the double-checked locking idiom on our method How do we do that

pages.title: Optimization 2: The Volatile Keyword
pages.subtitle: Understand the necessity of `volatile` to prevent partial object visibility issues.
pages.body:
now marked as volatile so every time we access
 this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
 So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user To avoid doing this and access the
 memory only once what we can do is
 store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
 then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
 to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory
keyTakeaways:
now marked as volatile so every time we access
 this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
 So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user

To avoid doing this and access the
 memory only once what we can do is
 store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
 then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement

The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
 to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory

This simple usage of such a local variable
 can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
 So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
 instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
 of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
 This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
 However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs
 to be adapted to handle multiple threads
pages.misconceptionFix: Do not memorize only the words. Understand why this concept is used and where it can fail.

 this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
 So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
 we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
 if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
 statement to return it to the user
pages.example:
This simple usage of such a local variable
 can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
 So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
 instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
 of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
 This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
 However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs
 to be adapted to handle multiple threads

summary: The Singleton Pattern ensures that a class has only one instance throughout the application, providing a single, controlled point of access. This guide covers the implementation steps, from basic usage to advanced multi-threading solutions using synchronized blocks, volatile keywords, and double-checked locking.
